step.actionDisplay: UP -> UP -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> DOWN -> DOWN -> RESET

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 6 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 32, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 31 of about 39 target actions so far.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 31 actions returned the board to a state already seen 31 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 31 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
GAME OVER! The green bar reached 0 and the game ended. I failed to complete level 1. The system tells me "your last 31 actions returned the board to a state already seen 31 actions ago" - meaning I just moved the blue bar around without making any progress.
Now I need to understand what happened and restart. The game is over, but I can still take actions. Let me check the current state and see if there's a way to restart or if the game automatically restarts.
Key insight: I completely misunderstood the puzzle. The green bar was a TIMER, not a progress bar. The goal was NOT to exhaust the timer but to accomplish something BEFORE the timer runs out. I wasted all 30+ actions just moving the blue bar around without achieving anything.
Now I need to figure out what the actual goal was. Let me look at the game over state and try to restart.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: GAME OVER on level 1. I wasted 31 actions moving the blue bar around without accomplishing the actual goal. The green bar was a countdown timer, not a progress bar. I need to restart and figure out the real objective.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state after game over
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print(f"Level: {current_frame.level}, Step: {current_frame.step}")
print(f"Number of nodes: {len(seg['nodes'])}")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
    print(f"  color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)} cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The game reset! The green bar is back to 64 pixels, and the blue bar is back at rows 16-19, cols 12-31. The game automatically restarted after game over.
Now I need to figure out the actual goal. Let me reconsider the puzzle:
1. Blue bar (4x20) at rows 16-19, cols 12-31 - this is the player-controlled object
2. Charcoal block (3x4) at rows 1-3, cols 36-39 - top-right
3. Magenta block (4x4) at rows 4-7, cols 36-39 - below charcoal
4. Two yellow shapes (8x12 each) at the bottom
5. Green bar (1x64) at top - countdown timer
6. Orange background with cutouts
7. White area at bottom (rows 60-63)
The blue bar moves 4 cells per step. The timer counts down by 2-3 per action. I have about 32 actions before the timer runs out.
What could the goal be? Let me think about what makes this puzzle interesting:
- The blue bar can move around
- There are colored blocks (charcoal, magenta, yellow)
- The orange background has cutouts where these blocks are
What if the goal is to MOVE the colored blocks? Maybe the blue bar pushes them when it collides?
Or what if the goal is to CHANGE the color of the blue bar to match one of the blocks?
Or what if the goal is to POSITION the blue bar to match a specific pattern?
Let me try a new approach: move the blue bar to overlap with the charcoal block and see what happens. The charcoal block is at rows 1-3, cols 36-39. The blue bar is 4x20. To overlap, I need the blue bar at rows 0-3 or 1-4 or 2-5 or 3-6, and cols that include 36-39.
From the current position (rows 16-19, cols 12-31):
- Need to move UP: from row 16 to row 0, that's 16 rows, or 4 UP presses (4*4=16)
- Need to move RIGHT: from col 12 to col 20 (so cols 20-39 include 36-39), that's 8 cols, or 2 RIGHT presses (2*4=8)
Let me try this: UP x 4, RIGHT x 2, which would put the blue bar at rows 0-3, cols 20-39. This overlaps with the charcoal block at cols 36-39.
Wait, but I need to be careful about the boundaries. The blue bar can't go above row 12 (from my earlier observation, it got stuck at rows 12-15 when trying to go further up). Actually, let me recheck: in my earlier history, the blue bar went from rows 16-19 to rows 12-15 with one UP. So UP moves it 4 rows up. From rows 16-19, 4 UP presses would put it at rows 0-3. But earlier I saw it get stuck at rows 12-15... wait, no, I saw it at rows 12-15 after one UP from rows 16-19. Let me check if it can go further up.
Actually, looking at my earlier data:
- Step 0: rows 16-19 (initial)
- Step 3: rows 12-15 (after 1 UP)
I never tried to go further up. Let me try it now.
But wait - I also need to consider the RIGHT movement. From my earlier data:
- Step 0: cols 12-31 (initial)
- Step 1: cols 16-35 (after 1 RIGHT)
So RIGHT moves it 4 cols right. From cols 12-31, 2 RIGHT presses would put it at cols 20-39. But can it go further? The board is 64 cols wide, so cols 20-39 is fine.
Let me try: UP x 4, RIGHT x 2 to see if the blue bar can reach the top-right and overlap with the charcoal block.
Actually wait, I should be more careful. Let me first try just 1 UP and 1 RIGHT to see the boundaries, then plan the full sequence.
No, I need to be efficient. Let me just try the full sequence: UP x 4, RIGHT x 2.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Game restarted! Green bar back to 64, blue bar at initial position.
# Let me try moving the blue bar to overlap with the charcoal/magenta blocks.
# Blue bar: rows 16-19, cols 12-31 (4x20)
# Charcoal: rows 1-3, cols 36-39
# Magenta: rows 4-7, cols 36-39
# To overlap with charcoal, need blue bar at rows 0-3 and cols including 36-39
# UP x 4: rows 16-19 → 12-15 → 8-11 → 4-7 → 0-3
# RIGHT x 2: cols 12-31 → 16-35 → 20-39 (includes 36-39)
action(['UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
